revision_note: Batch 7 (2026-07-23): Book III 'Crows and Owls' completion. Adds the 12 remaining canonical Ryder (1925) tales for Book III not previously ingested (refs 3.9-3.20), bringing Book III to full 17/17 canonical coverage (5 already ingested in chapter_3.json + 12 here). Sourced from theoceanofstories.blogspot.com individual story posts via the Panchatantra Index master page. Two nested/embedded tales included: 'The Cave That Talked' is told within the frame narrative, and 'The Butter-Blinded Brahman' is explicitly embedded within 'The Frogs That Rode Snakeback' (already ingested as 3.8 in chapter_3.json) -- both are separately titled, separately ingestable tales per established precedent (see Book V's Four Treasure-Seekers frametale in chapter_5.json). Confirms the established non-retrofit precedent: 'Mouse-Maid Made Mouse' (3.18) is a fresh, correctly-titled new reader entry (panchatantra-mouse-maid-made-mouse), left distinct from the previously-flagged non-matching entry panchatantra-sage-and-mouse, which remains untouched. Mature-content handling applied to 'The Gullible Carpenter' (3.17) and 'The Butter-Blinded Brahman' (3.20), both involving infidelity themes -- curated_lesson describes these discreetly as deception/manipulation without graphic or romantic detail, consistent with prior batches (Three-Breasted Princess, Farmer's Wife, Ungrateful Wife). 'The Self-Sacrificing Dove' (3.13) involves a self-immolation/devotional-sacrifice theme, retold as a moral fable about hospitality and transformation without instructional or graphic detail, consistent with app content-sensitivity guidance around self-harm depictions.

content.story_title: How the Birds Picked a King
content.text: There is on a certain mountain a banyan tree, where dwell many birds. Once, when their king Garuda failed to protect them, they assembled and resolved to have another king. "Garuda pays no heed to our sorrows; we suffer death and destruction with no protector; let us choose a king who will guard us in our need." And their eyes falling upon the owl, whose appearance was dignified, they said: "This owl shall be our king." And they made ready with pitchers of holy water, with a throne, with unhusked barley, with oil and lampwicks, with all the paraphernalia of a coronation. And the owl began to be crowned. But at this moment a crow arrived, thinking: "Whither shall birds fly for protection who wish to have such a wry-faced president as this, one who is ugly to see, and ill-tempered even in his everyday moods; how, if angry, would he glare at the person addressing him? And besides, this owl is blind by day. How could he ward off Garuda's attacks upon us, coming as they do in the day?" So he said: "What use is this coronation? Consider: he is hideous of aspect even in a natural state, and how much more so when angry. Blind by day, timid, ill-tempered -- how shall such a king save us from calamity?" Hearing this, the birds cast off their purpose and flew away, leaving the owl on his throne, wondering at their sudden departure.
content.curated_lesson: The bird clans, frustrated that their nominal king Garuda paid them no attention and never came to their aid, gathered to choose a real king who would actually protect them. Impressed by his solemn, dignified bearing, they settled unanimously on the owl and began an elaborate coronation ceremony. Just as the ritual was underway, a crow arrived and, curious about the commotion, was invited to give his opinion too, since he was known as the shrewdest of birds. He immediately objected: why crown a creature who is blind precisely during the day, when most danger arrives? Persuaded by this simple but crucial flaw no one else had considered, the birds abandoned the ceremony and flew off to reconsider, leaving the owl alone on his half-finished throne. When the owl learned that the crow's sharp question had cost him the crown, he swore lasting enmity against the crow.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: In a certain place there lived a great black snake named Haughty. And one day he thought: "I will not go by the path I have always used, for I am weary of it. I will use a narrower way." So thinking, he crawled with great effort into a very narrow crevice, and in the crush he was severely wounded. And the smell of blood brought a swarm of ants, who stung him from every side. Though he tried to defend himself, killing many, he could not withstand the multitude, and so perished, brought low not by any single powerful foe but by the countless small ones acting together.
content.curated_lesson: A great black snake named Haughty lived in an ant-hill, but one day, rather than take the wide path he normally used to leave his hole, he chose to force his massive body through a much narrower crack -- and in doing so, wounded himself badly. The smell of his blood drew the ants of the hill, who gathered around him in overwhelming numbers. No single ant posed him any threat, but their sheer collective mass, stinging from every side at once, was more than even a powerful snake could withstand. Haughty perished not from any one enemy strong enough to defeat him, but from an uncountable number of small ones acting together.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: In a certain town there lived a Brahman named Haridatta, whose fields yielded little despite his toil. One hot day, resting under a tree, he saw a great snake, hood raised, before an ant-hill nearby, and thought: "This must be the guardian deity of my field, unhonored by me until now; that is why my land does not prosper." So he brought a bowl of milk and offered it, bowing. The next morning he found a gold coin in the bowl's place, and daily thereafter the exchange continued, milk for gold. One day, called away, he sent his son to make the offering. The son, on finding a coin, reasoned that the ant-hill must be full of gold, and resolved to kill the snake and seize the treasure at once. He struck at it with a stick, but the snake escaped the blow and, biting him in self-defense, killed him instantly. The father, grieving, understood that his son's own greed, not the snake's malice, was the cause. Still, hoping to preserve the arrangement, the father returned the next day with the milk as always, and addressed the snake, saying his son had met the fate his own folly deserved, and hoping they might continue as before. But the snake replied: "I will continue the arrangement, since you ask it, but understand: a funeral pyre, once built, can be pulled down and moved; but a heart once wounded, however it is later patched together, retains the scar of the wound. Money you shall have, but never again the trust of former days."
content.curated_lesson: A Brahman farmer, whose land yielded little despite his labor, spotted an imposing snake near an ant-hill and, assuming it was the field's guardian deity gone unhonored, began offering it a saucer of milk every day, receiving a gold coin in return each morning. One day, called away on business, he sent his son to deliver the milk instead. Finding a coin, greed overtook the boy: reasoning the ant-hill must be full of gold, he tried to kill the snake outright, striking it with a club. The snake survived and struck back in self-defense, killing the boy instantly. The grieving father understood his son's own greed, not the snake's nature, had caused the tragedy. Hoping to preserve what remained of the relationship, he returned the next morning with milk as always -- but the snake gave an honest, sobering reply: a funeral pyre can be dismantled and moved, but a heart wounded and patched back together always carries the scar of what happened.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: There was a king named Chitraratha (Gay-Chariot) who owned a lake guarded well, where dwelt golden swans, each of whom, twice a year, gave up a feather of gold as a kind of rent for the protection they enjoyed. One day a great golden bird came seeking to dwell there also, but the resident swans refused him, saying: "This lake is ours by agreement with the king; there is no room here for one who has not shared in our arrangement." Insulted, the bird went to the king and reported the swans as rude and inhospitable. The king, without further inquiry, in anger ordered his men to kill every swan on the lake. An old swan, seeing the armed men approach, cried: "There is no time to argue or explain our case; let us flee together at once," and so they abandoned the lake and escaped with their lives, though they lost the home and privilege they had guarded so carefully -- undone by one refusal of hospitality to a single guest in need.
content.curated_lesson: A king named Gay-Chariot owned a well-guarded lake where golden swans lived, each contributing a valuable tail feather twice a year as a kind of unspoken rent for the protection they received. When a great golden bird arrived seeking to live there too, the resident swans refused him outright, citing their exclusive arrangement with the king. Insulted, the newcomer went straight to the king and reported the swans as rude and inhospitable. The king, without investigating further, angrily ordered his men to kill every bird on the lake. An old swan, spotting the armed men approaching, understood there was no time to argue -- their only chance was to abandon the lake immediately and flee together, which they did just in time to survive. What the swans lost was not their lives, but the home and privileged arrangement they had spent so much effort protecting -- undone in a single day by a refusal of basic hospitality to one guest in need.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: A hunter, feared by every creature of the forest, was caught one evening in a violent storm with no shelter to be found, and at last, shivering and desperate, took refuge beneath a great tree. In that tree dwelt a dove, who grieved aloud because his wife had not returned home in the storm, lamenting how empty a house feels without the one who makes it home. His wife, who was in fact caught in a fowler's cage not far off, heard his lament and, moved by his devotion, called to him urging that he treat the shivering stranger below as an honored guest, not an enemy, for her own captivity was the fruit of her own past deeds, not his fault, and to turn away a guest in need is a debt the soul itself must pay. Moved by this, the dove welcomed the hunter warmly, and built him a fire from a single surviving ember. But realizing he had nothing to offer his starving guest, he made an extraordinary choice: rather than fail in the duty of hospitality, he threw himself into the very fire he had kindled, offering his own body as the guest's meal. Witnessing this, the hunter was shattered with remorse, and on the spot broke every tool of his trade -- club, net, and cage -- freed the grieving widow-dove, and renounced hunting and all worldly pleasure forever, choosing a life of penance. The freed dove, finding her mate gone, walked into the same fire to join him in death, and the tale ends not in tragedy but in transcendence, the two doves reunited and honored, while the hunter's own renunciation, born of what he had witnessed, led him at last to peace.
content.curated_lesson: A hunter, feared by every creature in the forest, was caught in a violent storm one evening with no refuge anywhere, and finally sheltered, shivering and desperate, beneath a tree. In that tree lived a dove, grieving because his wife had not yet returned home in the storm. His wife, listening from a fowler's cage nearby where she had been trapped, urged him to treat the shivering stranger below as an honored guest rather than an enemy. Moved by this, the dove welcomed the hunter warmly, built him a fire, and then, having no food to offer, made an extraordinary choice: he threw himself into the fire, offering his own body as the guest's meal. Witnessing this act of total self-sacrifice, the hunter was shattered with remorse. He destroyed every tool of his trade, freed the grieving widow, and renounced hunting and worldly pleasure forever, choosing a life of penance. The freed dove, finding her mate gone, chose to join him by walking into the same fire -- and the tale ends not in tragedy but in transcendence, both doves reunited and honored.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: The Old Man with the Young Wife
content.text: An aging merchant, overtaken by loneliness after the death of his wife, paid a large sum to marry a much younger woman from a struggling family -- a match she could barely tolerate. One night a thief crept into their home. Startled, the young wife instinctively turned and embraced her husband for protection, the first real closeness she had shown him. The old man, delighted, but noticing the thief lurking in the corner, understood at once that her embrace was born of fear, not affection. Rather than resent this, he addressed the thief with wry gratitude, offering him anything he wanted as thanks for the moment of closeness his presence had accidentally caused. The thief declined with equal dry humor, but remarked that if he ever wanted something from the house, he would gladly return -- on condition that she still was not embracing her husband passionately by then.
content.curated_lesson: An aging merchant, so overtaken by loneliness after his wife's death that his judgment failed him, married a much younger woman from a struggling family -- a match the young bride could barely tolerate. One night, a thief crept into their home. Startled, the young wife instinctively turned and embraced her husband for protection -- the first real closeness she had shown him since their marriage. The old man, astonished and delighted, noticed the thief lurking in the corner and understood immediately: her embrace wasn't affection, but simple fear of the intruder. Rather than resent this, he addressed the thief with wry gratitude. The thief, with equal dry humor, declined -- but noted that if he ever wanted something from the house, he'd be glad to come back. The tale is remembered for its clear-eyed honesty about a mismatched marriage: the old man's own self-awareness, recognizing fear rather than mistaking it for love, keeps the story gently comic rather than purely sad.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: The Brahman, the Thief, and the Ghost
content.text: A poor Brahman, given two calves out of pity, raised them with such devoted care -- feeding them butter and oil he had begged for himself -- that they grew plump and valuable, catching the eye of a passing thief, who resolved to steal them. On his way to commit the theft that night, the thief met a hideous ghost, who introduced himself as one who ate but once every three days, and who was, by coincidence, planning to eat that very Brahman that same night. Delighted to find they shared a target, the two agreed to travel together. But once they arrived and the Brahman had fallen asleep, a dispute broke out: the ghost wished to eat the Brahman at once, while the thief insisted the cattle must be stolen first, since the ghost's attack would surely wake the man and ruin the theft -- and the reverse was equally true. Each accused the other of threatening to ruin his own plan, and their argument grew so loud that it woke the Brahman himself, who sat up to hear both the danger to his life and the danger to his cattle explained to him by his own would-be attackers, each blaming the other. Forewarned by their quarrel, he prayed to his protecting deity to save him from the ghost, then took up a club to drive off the thief and save his cows -- saved not by his own strength, but by the simple fact that his two enemies, pursuing different selfish ends, could not cooperate long enough to avoid exposing themselves.
content.curated_lesson: A poor Brahman, given two calves out of pity, raised them with such devoted care that they grew plump and valuable, catching the eye of a passing thief who resolved to steal them. On his way to commit the theft that night, the thief crossed paths with a hungry ghost who was, by pure coincidence, planning to eat that very same Brahman that same night. Delighted to find they were headed to the same target, the two agreed to travel together. But once they arrived, a dispute broke out over who should act first, since each attack would ruin the other's plan. Their argument grew so heated and loud that it woke the Brahman himself, who learned of both dangers from his own would-be attackers, each blaming the other. Forewarned by their quarrel, he prayed to his protective deity to save himself from the ghost, then took up a club to drive off the thief. What saved the Brahman was not his own strength, but the simple fact that his two enemies could not cooperate long enough to avoid exposing themselves.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: The Snake in the Prince's Belly
content.text: A prince, wasting away because a snake had secretly made its home in his belly instead of an ant-hill, left his kingdom in despair and lived as a beggar in a temple in a distant land. In that land a king had two daughters, one who greeted him only with flattering praise, and another who spoke plainly, greeting him simply with 'your just deserts' instead of empty compliment. Offended, the king in anger married this daughter to the first foreigner available -- the ailing, beggared prince -- as a punishment for her bluntness. But the princess accepted her fate with grace, treating her new husband with genuine devotion, and traveled with him to a distant city to begin their life. One day, while her husband rested with his head near an ant-hill, she returned from an errand to find two snakes arguing loudly -- the one living in her husband's belly, and a second, native to the ant-hill, each mocking the other's secret vulnerability. Overhearing every word from behind a branch, she learned the cure named aloud in their quarrel: black mustard for the one, boiling water for the other. Using exactly what she had overheard, the princess cured her husband completely, and in doing so gained access to the treasure the ant-hill snake had also revealed. She returned home with her once-ailing, now-healthy husband and a fortune besides, honored by the very father who had cast her out.
content.curated_lesson: A prince, wasting away because a snake had secretly made its home in his belly, left his kingdom in despair and lived as a beggar in a distant land. There, a king had two daughters -- one who flattered him, another who spoke plainly. Offended by her honesty, the king angrily married the plain-spoken daughter off to the ailing, beggared prince as a punishment. But the princess accepted her fate with grace, treating her new husband with genuine devotion. One day, while he rested near an anthill, she overheard two snakes arguing -- the one in her husband's belly, and one native to the anthill, each revealing the other's secret cure. Using what she overheard, the princess cured her husband completely and gained access to hidden treasure besides. She returned home with her now-healthy husband and a fortune, honored by the very father who had cast her out -- her honest tongue, once punished, having quietly earned her everything in the end.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: A carpenter, hearing persistent village gossip questioning his wife's faithfulness, devised a test: he announced a multi-day trip to another village, watched her prepare his travel food with suspicious eagerness, then secretly returned that same evening and hid beneath their own bed. His suspicion proved correct almost at once: his wife, believing him gone, invited an old lover to visit that very night. As the man climbed into bed, the carpenter beneath debated whether to strike or wait and watch, and chose to wait. When his wife, locking the door, accidentally stepped on her hidden husband, she realized instantly what was happening, and thinking quickly, invented a story on the spot: she claimed a goddess had appeared to her that very morning, warning that her husband was fated to die within six months unless she embraced another man, an act that would transfer the doomed fate onto him instead. Hearing this from his hiding place, the carpenter was completely won over -- not suspicious of the wife he had just caught, but grateful, believing he had witnessed devotion rather than betrayal. He emerged from under the bed praising her loyalty, and insisted on carrying both his wife and her lover triumphantly through the streets to celebrate what he believed was a life-saving sacrifice.
content.curated_lesson: A carpenter, having heard village gossip questioning his wife's faithfulness, devised a test: he announced a trip, then secretly returned that same evening and hid beneath their own bed to observe what would happen. His suspicions proved correct almost immediately -- his wife, believing him truly gone, invited an old lover to visit that very night. When she accidentally discovered her hidden husband, she thought quickly and invented an elaborate story: a goddess had warned her that her husband would die unless she embraced another man, transferring his fate onto him instead. Hearing this from his hiding place, the carpenter was completely won over -- believing he had witnessed genuine devotion rather than betrayal. He emerged praising her loyalty and insisted on celebrating what he believed was a life-saving sacrifice. The tale is remembered as a sharp caution about willful blindness: a clever enough story, offered at exactly the moment someone wants to believe something, can make a person celebrate the very thing they set out to catch.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: A holy hermit, performing his ritual ablutions by a river, caught a baby mouse fallen from a hawk's talons into his own hand. Moved by compassion, he used his spiritual power to transform her into a human girl and raised her as his daughter, since his wife had always longed for a child. When the girl reached the age for marriage, her devoted father, wanting only the best for her, summoned the sun himself as a potential husband. But the girl found the sun too hot and overwhelming, and asked for someone even greater. The sun named the cloud, who could hide even him. The girl rejected the cloud too, as too cold and dark, so the holy man asked the cloud who was greater still, and was told the wind. The wind proved too restless for her taste, so she asked for someone greater than him too, and the wind named the mountain, immovable and firm. But the mountain proved too rough and stiff for her liking, and when asked who exceeded even his own strength, the mountain gave a surprising answer: the mouse, who alone could burrow through and undermine even a mountain's solid mass. The moment the holy man summoned a mouse and presented him to his daughter, something in her responded instantly -- a recognition of her own kind that no grandeur in sun, cloud, wind, or mountain had produced. She asked her father to change her back into a mouse so she could live authentically with her own kind, and he granted her wish.
content.curated_lesson: A holy hermit rescued a baby mouse and, moved by compassion, transformed her into a human girl, raising her as his daughter. When she reached marrying age, he offered her in turn to the Sun, the Cloud, the Wind, and the Mountain, each claiming a superior was even stronger -- but she rejected all of them as unsuitable. When finally offered a mouse, she instantly recognized her own kind, a recognition none of the grand and powerful suitors had produced, and asked to be turned back into a mouse so she could live authentically and marry him.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: A starving lion, finding nothing to eat all day, discovered a large cave at sunset and hid inside it, reasoning that whatever animal lived there would eventually return home, giving him an easy meal. The cave's actual resident, a jackal, arrived at the entrance and called out his usual greeting, a habitual call-and-response ritual he had long kept with the cave itself, which normally echoed back a greeting whenever he returned. When no reply came, the jackal, suspicious, announced loudly that since the cave apparently would not greet him today, he would simply go find another that would. The hiding lion, not understanding this was a deliberate test, assumed the cave always greeted the jackal, and that answering would lure him close enough to catch. So the lion called out his own imitation of a greeting -- but his massive roar, amplified by the cave's natural echo, thundered out far louder and more terrifying than any cave-echo should have been. The jackal, far from fooled, understood exactly what was hiding inside and fled at once, reciting wryly that a long life in the forest had taught him many things, but he had never heard of a cave that talked back on its own.
content.curated_lesson: A starving lion, finding nothing to eat all day, discovered a large cave and hid inside it, hoping to ambush whoever lived there. The cave's actual resident, a jackal, arrived and called out his usual greeting -- a ritual he had long kept with the cave's natural echo. When no reply came, the jackal announced loudly that he'd go find another cave. The hiding lion, misunderstanding this as a signal that the cave always greeted the jackal, called out his own imitation of a greeting -- except his massive roar thundered out far louder than any cave-echo should have been. The jackal, far from fooled, immediately understood what was hiding inside and fled, reciting wryly that he had never heard of a cave that talked back on its own. What undid the lion's ambush was not being seen, but being heard too well.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text: [Inside "The Frogs That Rode Snakeback"] A Brahman named Theodore grew suspicious when he noticed his wife constantly preparing rich, expensive butter-cakes and carrying them off somewhere daily, and confronted her directly. She claimed, without missing a beat, that she had undertaken a fasting ritual at a nearby shrine and was carrying the cakes as an offering to the goddess -- a story convincing enough that he let her go, imagining the dishes were destined for divine worship rather than a purpose of her own. Unbeknownst to her, the Brahman followed by a different route and hid behind the very statue of the goddess she claimed to be worshipping. There he overheard his wife praying aloud to the goddess to somehow make him blind, and, disguising his voice to sound like the deity herself, he answered from his hiding place that feeding him butter-cakes every day would eventually cause exactly that. Believing she had received real divine instruction, the wife happily continued feeding her husband rich food daily, thrilled when he later mentioned his eyesight seemed to be failing -- while her secret lover, hearing the Brahman was now blind, grew bold enough to visit the house openly. The Brahman, having engineered the whole situation, waited until the moment was right, then caught the lover as he entered and dealt with the betrayal decisively, ending both the deception and the affair in one stroke.
content.curated_lesson: A wife secretly fed her husband rich butter-cakes daily, claiming she was fasting at a shrine, when in fact she was pleasing a secret lover. Growing suspicious, the Brahman followed her in secret and hid behind the very shrine statue she claimed to worship. There he overheard her praying to somehow make him blind -- and, disguising his voice as the goddess, answered that feeding him butter-cakes daily would achieve exactly that. Believing she had received divine instruction, she happily continued, thrilled when he later mentioned his eyesight failing -- while her emboldened lover began visiting openly, believing the husband was now blind. The Brahman, having engineered the whole situation, waited for the right moment, caught the lover as he entered, and dealt with the betrayal decisively -- proof that patient, careful observation can outlast even a well-constructed lie.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)
